content.full_text:
RAvI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES
COMMISSION
June 29,
1995
Diane Fishburn
Olson, Hagel,
Fong,
Leidigh, Waters and Fishburn
Plaza Towers
555 Capitol Mall,
Suite 1425
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1-95-194
Dear Ms.
Fishburn:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”)
1
regulating the appropriate use of campaign funds.
Since you have
not named the specific official on whose behalf the advice is
requested,
we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance.
2
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
add±bion, thisletter is based on the facts presented to us.
- The
-
oimuission does not act as the finderof fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000-18995.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-95-194
Page
2
OUESTION
May campaign funds be used to pay for speech lessons from a
professional speech coach to improve a candidate’s speaking skills
for the 1996 campaign?
CONCLUSION
Campaign funds may be used to pay for speech lessons so long
as the payment is directly related to a political purpose.
FACTS
You represent an elected state officeholder who will be a
candidate for election in 1996.
The officeholder asked whether
campaign funds may be used to hire a professional speech coach for
speech lessons for the officeholder.
The purpose of the lessons
is to improve the officeholder’s speaking skills for the 1996
campaign.
ANALYSIS
The general rule of the personal use law is that any
expenditure of campaign funds must be, at a minimum, reasonably
related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
(Section 89512.)
However, under some circumstances, the personal
use law requires a stricter standard.
For example, where an
expenditure confers a substantial personal benefit on the
candidate or officeholder, the expenditure must be directly
related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
3
(Section 89521.)
—
You have asked whether campaign funds can be used to hire a
professional-speech coach. _Section895l3(b) provides that
-
campaign funds shall not be used to pay for or reimburse the cost
of professional services unless the services are directly related
to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
You stated
that the speech coach would be hired by the candidate to provide
speech lessons to improve the candidate’s speaking skills for the
1996 cainpaign.
So long as the training is -solely for the purpose
of enhancing the candidate’s speaking skills for the future
campaign, there would be a direct relationship between the
expenditure of campaign funds for the lessons and a political
A substantial personal benefit is defined as a direct personal
benefit to the candidate or officeholder of $100 or more.
(Section 89511.)


Our File No.
l—9—1q
Page
3
purpose.
Consequently,
campaign funds may be used to pay for the
lessons.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322-566O.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
Counsel
Copies of Commission regulations. and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 1O per page.
John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May campaign funds be used to pay for speech lessons from a
professional speech coach to improve a candidate’s speaking skills
for the 1996 campaign?

CONCLUSION: Campaign funds may be used to pay for speech lessons so long
as the payment is directly related to a political purpose.
